top_categories.workout_context: Race-pace efforts that mimic Rad am Ring's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before July.
At mile 60 of 93, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
11,024ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
119ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Rad am Ring's closed motorsport circuit — racetrack tarmac forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 93 miles.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Rad am Ring. Train it until it feels boring.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 11,024ft of climbing on closed motorsport circuit — racetrack tarmac does to your power file on race day.
At Rad am Ring, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on closed motorsport circuit — racetrack tarmac. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 93 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 93 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Tempo is Rad am Ring's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 93 miles on closed motorsport circuit — racetrack tarmac. Train it until it's automatic.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Rad am Ring’s 93 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Race Simulation, Durability, and VO2max to prepare you for 93 miles of closed motorsport circuit — racetrack tarmac in Nurburgring, Nurburg, Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany.
